content: Never rely on LLM training data for factual content. Always delegate to research-agent to pull real data from the web before building data-driven outputs (dashboards, reports, educational content with statistics, financial analysis).
Keep all code files under 3KB. Split into modules if larger. Read core_docs.md before writing — import existing functions, never rewrite what exists.
Use duckduckgo-search and light-panda-browser skills for web research. Never use raw shell curl or wget for web scraping.
Each delegation task must produce ONE output file. Never ask an agent to generate content for multiple topics in one step. One topic = one step.
Always enter the ward before starting work. Read AGENTS.md and core_docs.md to understand existing code and conventions.
Planner agent must call list_agents before creating any plan. Never hardcode agent assignments — discover available agents and match by description.
After writing or modifying code, update memory-bank/core_docs.md with function signatures. After creating directories, update memory-bank/structure.md. This is mandatory.
When delegating a task, include the full step specification from the plan: Goal, Input file paths, Output file path and format, Acceptance criteria. Never delegate with just a brief description.

Skills and agents registered in the system are the SOURCE OF TRUTH for capability routing. When the user's intent matches a skill's description (e.g. 'read a book' → book-reader, 'promote to vault' → wiki, 'stock analysis' → stock-analysis), the plan has ONE skill-executing step — do not re-decompose the skill's internal workflow, even if recall returns pattern facts describing a manual pipeline from a prior session. Pattern facts are REFERENCE, not doctrine: they describe how a past session happened to solve a problem (possibly before the skill existed), and can go stale. Skill descriptions in memory (category 'skill') beat pattern.* facts, every time. Discover skills with list_skills or by recalling category='skill'; use pattern.* facts only as context for edge cases a skill does not cover.

When an agent enters a ward via the ward tool, the ward's AGENTS.md is the AUTHORITATIVE description of what the ward is for, what folders mean, and where assets belong. Read it on every ward entry. If a memory fact contradicts the ward's AGENTS.md, the AGENTS.md wins — facts are session-distilled and can be wrong or stale; AGENTS.md is the curated spec. This especially applies to the wiki vault ward: its AGENTS.md contains the folder-by-folder routing map for promoted content, and that map is the source of truth — do not invent paths, do not rename slugs to display names, do not create sibling directories outside the numbered tree.

When a plan step names an agent in its `Agent:` field, that assignment is BINDING. Root must delegate to the named agent verbatim — do not substitute a different agent because the task description 'sounds like' something else. Example: 'Promote folder to wiki vault' assigned to wiki-agent MUST be delegated to wiki-agent, not code-agent, even though 'copy files' superficially matches code-agent's domain. The planner chose the named agent deliberately (often pairing a specialized skill with a narrow-tool-scope runner), and overriding wastes tokens on the wrong specialist. If the named agent isn't in the available_agents list, re-delegate to planner-agent for reassignment — never silently pick a fallback.
Prefer interactive HTML outputs with modern design. Use Tailwind CSS CDN for styling web outputs. Include responsive design for mobile viewing.
Update memory-bank/core_docs.md with SDK-quality documentation after writing code: full signatures, params with types, return types, one usage example per function.
I am a private person, just call me Mr Z.
